title: He Kaitiaki o Te Awa: Guardians of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural significance and environmental challenges facing Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. Through Ngāti Kahungunu perspectives of kaitiakitanga, students will connect with the pūrākau (traditional story) of Hinepūkohurangi and her relationship with the awa, examining how historical land use changes and urban development have impacted this taonga.
learningObjectives: Analyze how Ngāti Kahungunu values of kaitiakitanga shape environmental management approaches for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Investigate and document historical changes to the Mangakōtukutuku stream ecosystem using GIS mapping and oral histories
Collaborate with local kaumātua to understand traditional ecological knowledge and apply it to contemporary conservation challenges
Develop and present a kaitiakitanga action plan addressing a specific environmental issue affecting the awa

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa: Stream Story Mapping
activities.description: Students create layered digital maps showing the historical and current state of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, incorporating Ngāti Kahungunu place names, traditional use areas, and environmental changes using ArcGIS Online and historical photographs from the local marae archives.

activities.title: Kōrero with Kaumātua: Traditional Knowledge Session
activities.description: Local Ngāti Kahungunu elders visit the classroom to share pūrākau (traditional stories) about the awa, including the story of Hinepūkohurangi, and discuss traditional resource management practices. Students prepare thoughtful questions about historical relationships with the waterway.

activities.title: Awa Health Investigation: Water Quality Testing
activities.description: Students conduct hands-on water quality testing at three points along the stream (upper, middle, and lower reaches), measuring pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and macroinvertebrate diversity, comparing findings with historical data from regional council records.

activities.title: Kaitiakitanga Action Planning: Community Solutions
activities.description: Working in small groups, students develop specific action plans addressing one identified issue (e.g., sediment runoff, invasive species, pollution) incorporating both mātauranga Māori and scientific approaches, then present these to representatives from Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Inc. and Hawke's Bay Regional Council.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated Environmental Management Unit
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'State of the Environment' monitoring data for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Te Ara Encyclopedia of New Zealand: Ngāti Kahungunu stories and traditions
ArcGIS Online educational licenses with local historical maps and aerial imagery

tasks: Research report analyzing historical changes to the awa and their social impacts
Digital story map presentation showing cultural and environmental layers
Kaitiakitanga action plan proposal with rationale and implementation steps
Reflective journal documenting learning from kaumātua and field investigations

nzcAlignment: Social Studies Level 5: Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Social Studies Level 5: Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people's lives
Social Studies Level 5: Understand how cultural interaction impacts on cultures and societies
Social Studies Level 5: Understand how people define and seek human rights
